metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC and a named test (`test_always_sync`) specifying the observable behavior of `overlap_drain='always'` (e.g. drain runs synchronously in the calling process, `subprocess.Popen` is NOT called, and the return is blocking) so the mode has a concrete proving command.
findings.finding: The `overlap_drain='always'` mode (synchronous inline drain) is described in the What section as in-scope but has no acceptance criterion and no named test case in the Testing section, so an executor has no way to verify this mode is correctly implemented.
findings.location: What section — CLI description ('always = synchronous inline drain for tests/CI'); Testing section (no test for always mode); ACs (no AC for always mode)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the claim that acli_subprocess.py shows 'the established codebase pattern' for Windows DETACHED_PROCESS detach (it doesn't — it branches os.name for POSIX killpg only); instead, explicitly specify the Windows detach implementation inline (e.g. provide a concrete snippet showing `if os.name == 'nt': kwargs['creationflags'] = subprocess.DETACHED_PROCESS | subprocess.CREATE_NO_WINDOW` with a note that these are new-to-codebase constants).
findings.finding: The plan asserts acli_subprocess.py provides 'the established codebase pattern' for os.name-branched Windows detach (DETACHED_PROCESS|CREATE_NO_WINDOW), but the actual acli_subprocess.py os.name branch (line 360) only guards `start_new_session=True` for POSIX killpg reaping — a completely different purpose. Neither DETACHED_PROCESS nor CREATE_NO_WINDOW appears anywhere in the codebase, so there is no established pattern for implementers to follow.
findings.location: What section — 'Detach, cross-platform (DERISKED at API level)': 'Windows is therefore NEW work, branched on os.name (the established codebase pattern; cf. acli_subprocess.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Detach, cross-platform' bullet, replace '(the established codebase pattern; cf. acli_subprocess.py)' with '(new to this codebase — no prior Windows detach precedent exists; the acli_subprocess.py os.name branch guards start_new_session for killpg, a different concern)' and add a minimal inline code sketch so implementers have a concrete reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` to the Scope's edit targets: it needs an `enrich` intercept arm in `main()` (mirroring the existing `review`/`review-plan`/`workflow` intercepts) and a corresponding import of the handler from `_llm_commands.py`.
findings.finding: `_cli/__init__.py:main()` is an unenumerated consumer: every native LLM subcommand (`review`, `review-code`, `review-plan`, `workflow`, `llm`, etc.) requires an explicit intercept arm in `main()`, but the plan's Scope section names only `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` and omits `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` as a required edit target.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:main()
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, append: 'intercept arm for `enrich` in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:main()` (mirrors the `review`/`review-plan` pattern); import of `_enrich` added to the `from rebar._cli._llm_commands import ...` block.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (or close) the coverage gap: `transition`, `claim`, `compact`, and `delete` call `push_after_commit` directly and bypass the `write_and_push` injection point — either add `maybe_drain()` to `push_after_commit` in `push.py` (making it truly universal) or document the intentional narrower scope in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan claims a 'single authoritative site' for `maybe_drain()` in `event_append.write_and_push`, but `transition_close`, `claim`, `compact`, and `delete` all call `push.push_after_commit()` / `push_tickets_branch()` directly — bypassing `write_and_push` entirely — and are therefore unenumerated consumers that will never trigger `maybe_drain`.
findings.location: maybe_drain injection point section / src/rebar/_store/event_append.py:write_and_push
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `maybe_drain()` call into `push.push_after_commit()` (or `push_tickets_branch()`) rather than `write_and_push`, since `push_after_commit` is the single function all commit-then-push paths converge on. Update the 'injection point' section accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `maybe_drain()` is also wired into the `push_after_commit` seam in `push.py` (or equivalently into each of `claim`, `transition_close`, `compact`, `delete`) so the opportunistic drain fires for ALL write-command paths, not only those routed through `event_append.write_and_push`.
findings.finding: The `maybe_drain()` injection point in `event_append.py` misses all inline-commit write paths (`claim`, `transition`, `compact`, `delete`) that call `push.push_after_commit()` directly and never pass through `write_and_push`. If one of these commands is the last write in a user session, the opportunistic drain never fires.
findings.location: ## maybe_drain injection point (decided — no 'or') / src/rebar/_store/event_append.py / src/rebar/_store/push.py (push_after_commit)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `maybe_drain(base_path)` to `push.push_after_commit()` immediately after the `push_tickets_branch(...)` call — this is the natural symmetric site and covers all inline-commit callers in one place, mirroring the event_append pattern exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact behavior of `maybe_drain()` when `overlap_drain=always`: specify whether it calls the drain loop function directly in-process (e.g., `drain_batch(cfg)`) or invokes a subprocess synchronously, and add a corresponding test (`test_always_inline`) to lock in the contract.
findings.finding: The `overlap_drain` enum names three values (`off|async|always`) but only `off` and `async` have behavioral definitions in `maybe_drain()`; the `always` branch is labeled 'synchronous inline drain for tests/CI' but the plan never specifies what `maybe_drain` actually executes when `overlap_drain=always` — does it call the drain loop directly in-process, invoke `subprocess.run` (blocking), or something else? An implementer building the `always` branch of `maybe_drain` has no behavioral contract.
findings.location: What section — `overlap_drain ∈ {off, async, always}` paragraph; `maybe_drain injection point` section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `What` / `maybe_drain injection point` section: 'When `overlap_drain=always`, `maybe_drain` calls `drain_batch(cfg)` directly in-process (synchronous, blocking) — no subprocess spawn. This is the test/CI seam that exercises the full drain path without process isolation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `ENQUEUE_ENRICH`, `CLAIM_ENRICH`, and `DONE_ENRICH` must be added to `EVENT_TYPES` (write allow-list in `event_append.py`) AND to `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` in `_version.py` (following the `REVIEW_RESULT` precedent for non-reducer queue-management events), and include this registration as an explicit Scope item.
findings.finding: The three new event types `ENQUEUE_ENRICH`, `CLAIM_ENRICH`, and `DONE_ENRICH` are referenced throughout the drainer loop but are absent from both `EVENT_TYPES` (the write-path allow-list in `event_append.py`) and `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES` / `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (the reducer's forward-compat sets in `_version.py`). The plan never specifies that these types must be registered in these sets, nor which set they belong to. Since `stage_and_commit` validates `event_type` against `EVENT_TYPES` and raises `StoreError` for unknown types, the drainer's `DONE_ENRICH` append will fail at runtime without this registration.
findings.location: What — Drainer loop; Scope — `src/rebar/llm/enrich_drain.py`; codebase: `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py:38-65`, `src/rebar/reducer/_version.py:44,65`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Register `ENQUEUE_ENRICH`, `CLAIM_ENRICH`, `DONE_ENRICH` in `EVENT_TYPES` (`_store/event_append.py`) and `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` (`reducer/_version.py`) — these are queue-management sidecars (non-replay, like `REVIEW_RESULT`) so they are preserved-and-ignored by the state reducer and do not enter compiled state.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `prune` operation: specify exactly which event files are removed (e.g., all `ENQUEUE_ENRICH`/`CLAIM_ENRICH`/`DONE_ENRICH` files for a ticket except those belonging to the most recent completed drain cycle), the mechanism (git rm + commit inside a lock), and the function signature/module location.
findings.finding: The `prune` operation ("prune the ticket's queue events, keep the latest cycle") is referenced as a required post-drain step that bounds log growth, but the plan never defines: (a) what constitutes a 'cycle' (which specific event files to delete — all `ENQUEUE_ENRICH`/`CLAIM_ENRICH`/`DONE_ENRICH` except the latest set?), (b) the mechanism (a new function? git rm + commit?), and (c) where it lives in the module structure. The `reducer/` module has no `prune` function for this purpose (confirmed by grep). Without a definition, two implementers will produce incompatible prune behaviors.
findings.location: What — Drainer loop: 'prune the ticket's queue events (keep the latest cycle) so log growth is bounded'; Testing — `test_batch_cap` 'observing DONE + a post-drain pending count of 0'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the queue reducer API surface needed by `rebar enrich status`: either define that `pending_enrichment(now)` returns a structured object with `{pending, claimed, soaking}` counts (making it the single query for status), or name the three separate reducer functions/projections the status command calls.
findings.finding: The `status` subcommand (`rebar enrich status`) emits `{pending: int, claimed: int, soaking: int}` derived from 'the queue reducer', but the plan never specifies the return schema of `pending_enrichment(now)` or whether `status` calls one function with three return values vs. three separate reducer queries. This is a co-location gap: the processing protocol for the status command is not defined alongside the schema it needs.
findings.location: What — CLI `rebar enrich`: 'status (prints JSON {"pending":int,"claimed":int,"soaking":int} from the queue reducer)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and a corresponding test, e.g. `test_always_inline`) that verifies `overlap_drain=always` runs the drain synchronously in-process before the command returns.
findings.finding: The `overlap_drain = always` mode (synchronous inline drain for tests/CI) is described in 'What' but has no covering Acceptance Criterion or named test.
findings.location: What section: 'always = synchronous inline drain for tests/CI'; Acceptance Criteria (no AC for `always` mode)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs for `rebar enrich --once` (single in-process claim+enrich verified by `test_once`) and `rebar enrich status` (JSON output shape verified by a named test).
findings.finding: `rebar enrich --once` and `rebar enrich status` are described in the 'What' section CLI arm but have no covering Acceptance Criteria; `test_once` appears in Testing but is orphaned from any AC.
findings.location: What section (CLI arm); Testing section (`test_once`); Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'AC7: `rebar enrich --once` claims and processes exactly one soaked entry in-process and exits 0; `::test_once`. AC8: `rebar enrich status` prints valid JSON with keys `pending`, `claimed`, `soaking`; verified by a new `test_status` unit test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one test for `rebar enrich status` that asserts the JSON output contains the correct `pending`, `claimed`, and `soaking` integer fields (happy-path), and one that covers the behavior when the queue is empty.
findings.finding: The `rebar enrich status` subcommand (which prints JSON `{"pending":int,"claimed":int,"soaking":int}`) is listed in the CLI spec but has no corresponding test in the Testing section or Acceptance Criteria.
findings.location: ## What — CLI `rebar enrich`: `status` subcommand / ## Testing
findings.suggested_fix: Add `test_status_output` to `tests/unit/test_enrich_drain.py`: seed a known queue state (e.g., 2 pending, 1 claimed, 0 soaking), invoke `rebar enrich status`, and assert the parsed JSON matches exactly. Add a corresponding AC line: '`rebar enrich status` outputs valid JSON with correct integer counts; verified by `::test_status_output`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one integration/smoke test or AC that exercises the actual `rebar enrich --drain` subprocess end-to-end against a local (file-based) queue with real S1/S2/S3 fakes — OR explicitly defer this to a named integration story with a rationale sentence in the plan.
findings.finding: The default `async` detached drain path — the primary new behavior — is only exercised by mocked unit tests (FakeRunner, mocked subprocess). No end-to-end or semi-integration test exercises the actual subprocess spawn, detach, and drain against real queue/enrich dependencies, and no rationale or named child story explicitly defers this live-path exercise.
findings.location: Testing section; AC for maybe_drain ('detaches a surviving child otherwise'); Dependencies note ('S1/S2/S3 are prerequisite sibling stories, not existing code')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the stated rationale for placing overlap_drain on LLMConfig: replace 'NOT VerifyConfig (which is bool-only via _as_bool)' with an accurate reason (e.g. 'NOT VerifyConfig, which is a gate-enforcement config unrelated to LLM operational scheduling'), since VerifyConfig already has float, int, and str-tuple fields.
findings.finding: The plan states 'VerifyConfig (which is bool-only via _as_bool)' as the reason overlap_drain must live on LLMConfig rather than VerifyConfig. This premise is factually incorrect: VerifyConfig has float fields (verify_window_headroom, novelty_drop_threshold, novelty_priority_floor, completion_priority_floor), int fields (remediation_window_minutes), and str-tuple fields (completion_preserve_criteria), all with non-bool coercers in _SECTIONS.
findings.location: What section — 'overlap_drain is a STRING enum (off|async|always) so it lives on LLMConfig (read via from_env), NOT VerifyConfig (which is bool-only via _as_bool)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'NOT VerifyConfig (which is bool-only via _as_bool)' to 'NOT VerifyConfig (which is a gate-enforcement / workflow-gating concern; LLM scheduling/drain settings belong on LLMConfig alongside model, timeout, etc.)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `maybe_drain()` should fire at most once per top-level command invocation (e.g. via a per-process flag set after first spawn) or once per `write_and_push` call, and update the spec and `test_gate_latency` AC to reflect the actual scope of the 20ms budget.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify how `maybe_drain()`'s DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS check is scoped when `write_and_push` is called multiple times within a single user command (e.g. a batch-create that appends N events). As specified, the gate check runs once per `write_and_push` call, not once per command, meaning up to N concurrent drain children could be spawned and race the drain lock — each beyond the first exits immediately, but the plan's 20ms latency budget is consumed N times.
findings.location: ## maybe_drain injection point / ## Constants (defaults stated)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add non-happy-path specs for `rebar enrich status` (missing/empty queue → prints `{"pending":0,...}` or exits with clear error) and `--once` (empty queue → exit 0 + message; enrich failure → non-zero exit + logged error).
findings.finding: The `rebar enrich status` subcommand and `--once` mode specify only their happy-path behavior. The plan does not describe error states for status (queue file missing, corrupted, or locked) or for --once (nothing to process, enrich failure, lock contention). Since --once is the in-process test target, its exit-code/exception contract on failure is load-bearing for the test suite.
findings.location: What — CLI `rebar enrich`: '--once (claim+process one entry, in-process, tests)' and 'status (prints JSON ...)'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the fallback scan also catches tickets with a fresh TICKET_DIGEST but no DONE_ENRICH (fresh-digest-but-no-DONE path), or reverse the write order (DONE_ENRICH first, then TICKET_DIGEST) with idempotent re-enrichment.
findings.finding: The partial-write scenario — TICKET_DIGEST written but DONE_ENRICH not yet appended (child crashes between S2 write and queue append) — is not addressed for idempotency. The fallback scan targets 'certified but absent/stale digest' tickets, which would NOT match a ticket that already has a fresh TICKET_DIGEST. Such a ticket would be invisible to both the normal pending_enrichment() path (no DONE_ENRICH) and the fallback scan (digest is present and fresh), causing it to stall permanently.
findings.location: What — drainer loop: 'write TICKET_DIGEST (S2), append DONE_ENRICH' / 'fallback scan (tickets certified but with absent/stale digest)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define (or require S3 to define) a stale-lock detection/recovery mechanism (e.g. PID-stamped lock with liveness check, or a lock TTL) so a crash-while-holding scenario does not permanently disable draining.
findings.finding: The plan does not address stale-lock recovery: if a detached drain child crashes (SIGKILL, OOM, power loss) while holding the drain lock, all subsequent drain attempts will silently exit 0 forever. The plan specifies 'lock.acquire → exit 0' as the live-contention path but provides no lock-staleness detection (e.g. PID-stamped lock, lock TTL, or lock age check).
findings.location: What — 'lock.acquire, per S3' / AC: 'Concurrent drains don't double-process (lock-held → silent exit 0, logged)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that queue-event pruning is intentionally irreversible (or provide a rollback mechanism such as a pre-prune snapshot or an 'undo' append-only tombstone), and justify why the 'keep latest cycle' policy is safe for all consumers.
findings.finding: The `prune` of ticket queue events is an irreversible data operation acknowledged only as a growth-bounding measure, with no rollback path or explicit justification of its destructiveness.
findings.location: What section (Drainer loop); AC6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state after a `prune` commit (e.g., 'pruned events remain recoverable via `git log` on the tracker repo; queue reducers must tolerate their absence from HEAD'), and specify a compensating-forward path or explicit DATA LOSS RISK note if rollback after prune is accepted as a one-way door.
findings.finding: The `prune` step (git rm + commit of old queue events) is a destructive-to-HEAD operation with no stated back-out path and no assertion on post-rollback schema state. After prune commits, those queue event files are absent from HEAD; if the code is rolled back, older code expecting those events for replay will not find them in the working tree. The plan never specifies: (a) whether pruned events are recoverable by consumers, (b) what the schema/store state looks like after a code rollback from a post-prune HEAD, or (c) a compensating-forward strategy.
findings.location: What — Drainer loop: 'prune the ticket's queue events (keep the latest cycle) so log growth is bounded'; AC line: 'Each processed ticket gets a DONE_ENRICH append + a prune of its queue events'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section: 'Post-prune rollback: pruned ENQUEUE_ENRICH/CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH files are permanently absent from HEAD after prune. Code rollback after prune is a one-way door at the HEAD level; recovery requires `git revert` of the prune commit on the tracker repo. This is accepted because these are reducer-ignored events and the DONE_ENRICH marker is the durable completion signal.' Alternatively, defer prune to a separate bounded-growth story and omit it from this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the config field names: state explicitly which TOML key and which Python constant correspond (e.g., `overlap_drain_batch` (TOML/LLMConfig attr) backed by module-level `DRAIN_BATCH` default), and use those names consistently in both sections.
findings.finding: The config field names for the batch size and gate-budget constants are inconsistent between 'Constants' and 'Scope': 'Constants' uses bare names (`DRAIN_BATCH`, `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS`) while 'Scope' uses prefixed names (`overlap_drain_batch`, `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms`), leaving it ambiguous whether these are the same settings.
findings.location: Constants section; Scope section (config fields on LLMConfig)
findings.suggested_fix: In Constants, write: '`DRAIN_BATCH = 5` (default for `LLMConfig.overlap_drain_batch`), `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = 20` (default for `LLMConfig.overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms`)' to make the two-level naming explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Mitigate the store→llm cross-layer coupling in `event_append.py` by introducing an interface or registration hook (e.g., a `_drain_hook: Optional[Callable] = None` that `enrich_drain` registers at startup) so the store layer has no import-time dependency on the llm module.
findings.finding: The store layer (`event_append.py`) directly calls `maybe_drain()` from the LLM layer (`enrich_drain.py`), creating a downward dependency from a storage primitive into a high-level LLM feature. No interface, callback, or event boundary is proposed to mitigate this coupling, and the dependency direction is not acknowledged or justified architecturally.
findings.location: Scope: 'the maybe_drain hook in src/rebar/_store/event_append.py'; maybe_drain injection point section
findings.suggested_fix: In event_append.py, define `_post_push_hook: Optional[Callable[[], None]] = None`. In enrich_drain.py (or llm package init), register `event_append._post_push_hook = maybe_drain` at import time. event_append calls `if _post_push_hook: _post_push_hook()`. This keeps the store layer free of llm imports.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an explicit timeout on the enrich/LLM call (or require S3's lease TTL to be referenced and bounded here) so a hung LLM call is bounded and the claim is released predictably.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no timeout on the LLM/enrich call inside the detached child. If the LLM hangs indefinitely, the child process never exits, the claim is never released, and S3's lease-expiry TTL (defined in a sibling story, not this plan) is the only recovery path — but this plan neither references that TTL nor requires it. A hung child permanently reduces available drain concurrency and may accumulate over many invocations.
findings.location: What — drainer loop / 'enrich exception (LLM timeout/rate-limit) releases the claim (lease expiry)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `DRAIN_ENRICH_TIMEOUT_S` constant (e.g. 120 s) to LLMConfig; wrap the `enrich()` call in a `concurrent.futures` or `asyncio.wait_for` with that timeout; treat TimeoutError identically to other enrich exceptions (log + release claim + continue batch).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the 'maybe_drain injection point' section, enumerate the known callers of `event_append` (or the write-command path that leads to it) and explicitly confirm each is safe with the new `maybe_drain()` side-effect.
findings.finding: The `maybe_drain()` hook is injected into `event_append.py` — a write-command path — adding a new side-effecting behavior (potentially spawning a child process) to all callers of that module, but the plan does not name or acknowledge those callers to confirm they are safe with the new behavior.
findings.location: ## maybe_drain injection point (decided — no 'or') / Scope: `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'event_append is called by [list callers, e.g., rebar record, rebar push, rebar review]. Each caller is safe: the gate no-ops when nothing is soaked (O(1) read), and `overlap_drain=off` disables spawning entirely for constrained environments.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare a redaction posture for `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log`: either (a) install a logging filter that strips `Authorization`/`Bearer`/`api_key` patterns from log records before writing, or (b) document explicitly that exception text is sanitised before logging (e.g. log `str(type(exc))` + a truncated message rather than `repr(exc)` / full traceback).
findings.finding: The detached child's stderr is redirected to `.rebar/logs/enrich-drain.log` and the plan says `enrich` exceptions are logged there, but no redaction posture is declared. LLM HTTP-client exception tracebacks (httpx, aiohttp) can embed Authorization-header fragments or full bearer tokens from failed requests, making the new log file a plaintext-secret persistence point on the outbound LLM-API boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Observability bullet: 'the child configures a RotatingFileHandler … redirects its own stderr there'; Drainer loop bullet: 'an enrich exception … is logged'
findings.suggested_fix: In the per-item error-handling spec, replace 'exception … is logged' with 'exception type and a sanitised message (no repr of the HTTP response or request headers) is logged'; add a note that the RotatingFileHandler log filter strips any string matching known secret patterns (Bearer token, sk-, api_key=) before writing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State concrete default values for `RotatingFileHandler` parameters (e.g. `maxBytes=1_000_000`, `backupCount=3`) in the Constants section so the executor need not invent them.
findings.finding: The RotatingFileHandler log bounds are described as 'maxBytes+backupCount bounded' but the actual values are not specified anywhere in the plan. An executing agent must invent these numbers.
findings.location: What section — Observability bullet: 'configures a logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler (maxBytes+backupCount bounded)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Constants: `ENRICH_LOG_MAX_BYTES = 1_000_000` (1 MB), `ENRICH_LOG_BACKUP_COUNT = 3` — matching the pattern used by other bounded rotate handlers in the codebase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or update CLAUDE.md / a design doc) capturing: why opportunistic detached drain vs daemon/thread/scheduler, the Windows vs POSIX branching rationale, the store-layer injection point choice, and the overlap_drain enum semantics — so the decision is discoverable by future maintainers.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-cutting architectural pattern — opportunistic detached drain with cross-platform process orphaning — but mentions no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture the decision. Future maintainers have no authoritative record of why this pattern was chosen over alternatives (daemon, scheduler, in-process background thread).
findings.location: Why / What sections; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Demote `overlap_drain_batch` and `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` from `LLMConfig` fields to module-level constants in `enrich_drain.py` (e.g. `DRAIN_BATCH = 5`, `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS = 20`) and promote them to config only if/when a second call-site materialises (Rule-of-Three).
findings.finding: `overlap_drain_batch` and `overlap_drain_gate_budget_ms` are proposed as user-facing `LLMConfig` fields (with `[tool.rebar.llm]` file keys, env vars, and `from_env` resolution logic) but each has exactly one consuming call-site (`enrich_drain.py`), below the Rule-of-Three threshold for promoting an implementation constant to a shared config surface.
findings.location: Constants section; Scope section (`config fields … on LLMConfig`)
findings.suggested_fix: Keep `DRAIN_BATCH` and `DRAIN_GATE_BUDGET_MS` as module-level constants in `enrich_drain.py`. A single optional env-var override (e.g. `REBAR_ENRICH_DRAIN_BATCH`) read directly in the module is sufficient for CI/test overrides without touching `LLMConfig`'s shared surface.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
